title: Business Analysis & Problem Finding (IS 265)
power_phrases: Brooks's Law (adding people to a late project makes it later)
Reciprocity, commitment, social proof, authority, liking, scarcity
frameworks: 5 Whys: Toyoda's root cause analysis — ask why five times to get past symptoms to causes
Jobs to Be Done: Christensen's framework — what job is the user hiring this product to do?
Business Model Canvas: Osterwalder's one-page framework — value proposition, segments, channels, revenue, costs
Customer Development: Blank's four steps — Discovery, Validation, Creation, Building
Cialdini's 6 Principles of Persuasion: reciprocity, commitment, social proof, authority, liking, scarcity
Lean Startup: Build-Measure-Learn, hypothesis testing, validated learning
Brooks's essential vs. accidental complexity: the deepest insight in software engineering
concepts: Most failed projects fail because someone built an excellent solution to the wrong problem
Problem Finding: the ability to see the real problem hiding behind the stated one
'Jobs to Be Done' costs 5 tokens and activates Christensen, Harvard, the milkshake study, and an entire innovation theory
Describing the same concept from scratch costs 20+ tokens and produces a fuzzier response
Every power phrase is a capital investment: a few tokens yielding outsized returns in clarity
Pivoting is Problem Finding in action (PayPal Mafia lesson)
Fermi estimation: from 'I don't know' to 'my best estimate with stated assumptions is...'
ROI arithmetic: if the system costs $50K and saves $2K/month, payback at 25 months
context: IS 265 is Studio 4 (midpoint) of the BSEAI degree. Students shift from building technology to understanding the human and business problems technology is supposed to solve. Through real stakeholder interviews, process mapping, requirements elicitation, and business case analysis, students develop Problem Finding — the ability to see the real problem hiding behind the stated one.
